title: Quantum statistics in an extended collider coupled to a qubit
abstract: Mesoscopic colliders provide an effective platform for probing the mutual statistics of quantum particles. Recent experiments have successfully extracted the mutual statistics of fermions, and more exotic anyons using quantum point contacts (QPCs). Coupling a point-like collider, such as a quantum point contact, to a two-level impurity or qubit can induce statistical transmutation of fermions, causing them to display boson-like bunching tendencies. Here, we extend the analysis to an extended collider. We investigate the scattering of two incoming fermionic and bosonic wave packets in the presence of post-selection on the impurity state, and systematically analyze the possible benchmarks used to characterize bunching and infer the underlying mutual statistics. We show that only a specific benchmark faithfully captures the mutual statistics of the colliding particles, while alternative choices can produce spurious statistical signatures. Hence, the correct benchmark for probing the quantum statistics depends on the intricate details of the mesoscopic collider.
summary: The paper investigates how coupling an extended mesoscopic collider to a qubit affects the measurable mutual statistics of incoming particles. By analyzing multiple theoretical benchmarks against measured probabilities, the authors rigorously prove that only one specific benchmark correctly extracts the true quantum statistical signature. This highlights the critical dependence of physical inference on the precise measurement protocol used in nanoscale quantum experiments.
main_problem: The study aims to determine the correct diagnostic benchmark for extracting the mutual quantum statistics (bosonic vs. fermionic) of colliding particles when the scattering process is mediated by an extended mesoscopic collider coupled to a qubit.
main_result: Only a specific benchmark, $B_4 = S(11; |\psiangle)CWP(11)CW$, faithfully captures the true mutual statistics, as other choices yield spurious results. This confirms that the correct diagnostic tool depends intricately on the collider's geometry and coupling details.
method: The analysis involves deriving scattering matrices ($S_{	ext{eff}}$) for single and two-particle wave packets in the presence of a qubit, calculating post-scattering density matrices ($ho_{	ext{sys}}$), and systematically comparing multiple theoretical benchmarks ($B_1$ through $B_4$).
model_or_system: The system is an extended mesoscopic collider (modeled with tunneling points separated by L) coupled to a two-level impurity qubit. Scattering processes are analyzed for incoming fermionic and bosonic wave packets.
key_observables: Mutual statistics (bunching/anti-bunching), single-particle transmission/reflection probabilities ($P(1 	o 1)$, $P(1 	o 2)$), and the two-particle probability difference, $P(11; |\psiangle)F - B_4$.
important_parameters: Orbital phase $\phi = kL$, qubit coupling parameters ($\eta_m, 	heta_m$), and the state of the post-selected qubit $|\psiangle$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that incoming particles interact only via statistical interaction (neglecting Coulomb interactions) and relies on approximations for calculating correlation terms in the continuum limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the three physical setups: point-like, extended, and qubit-coupled. Figure 2 plots differences between calculated probabilities and benchmarks ($B_1, B_2$) to demonstrate statistical transmutation.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the effective scattering matrices for the coupled system (Eqs. 1-3), then calculating single-particle and two-particle probabilities dependent on the qubit state ($ho_{	ext{sys}}$). The core of the work is a systematic comparison of multiple statistical benchmarks ($B_1$ to $B_4$) to isolate the physically meaningful signature.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical framework for interpreting quantum transport measurements in nanoscale devices, showing how measurement choice (the 'benchmark') can fundamentally alter the inferred physical statistics, which is crucial knowledge for experimentalists using mesoscopic tools.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Mesoscopic colliders with two sources S1, S2 and two detectors D1, D2. A part of the incoming wave packet (grey- colored) is transmitted to the detector D1, transmitted wave packet (blue-colored) and other part of the incoming wave packet is reflected to the detector D2, reflected wave packet (red-colored). (a) Point-like collider with transmission and reflection amplitude given by Eq. (1). The transmitted (reflected) wave packet involves a single wavelet on the lead with detector D1 (D2). (b) Extended collider with effective transmission and reflection amplitude given by Eq. (2). In this case the reflected and the transmitted wave packet involves an infinite number of wavelets,...
